root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Intake Guide
root.summary: Front matter for the Cúram 8.1.2 Intake Guide. It identifies the edition as applying to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, and points readers to the Notices section before use. The main content introduces Cúram Intake as a flexible “No Wrong Door” framework for triage, screening, applications, and referrals across multiple programs. It covers client identification and registration, triage outcomes, screening for potential eligibility, the lifecycle of an application case (creation through closure, statuses, eligibility, authorization, PCR assessment, timers, and contact management), intake issues (verification, evidence validation, authorization failures), referrals to external agencies or service providers, and worker tools such as Advisor, Notes, Application Search, My Applications, Referral Search, My Referrals, and My Programs Due. The final pages contain Merative notices, warranty/export disclaimers, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the “Notices” section on page 41 before using the product or the information in this document. It also includes the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Cúram Intake edition for versions 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2. This section introduces the intake framework and its “No Wrong Door” approach, covering triage, screening, applications, and referrals. It outlines client identification and registration, triage questions/results, recommendations and alerts, mapping provider services, and referrals to community services or government programs. It also previews application case handling, eligibility, authorizations, issues, and worker tools like Advisor, Notes, and search views.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section covers Cúram Intake from triage and screening through application case creation and management. It explains how workers screen prospects or registered persons for potential program eligibility, interpret recommendations/alerts, and proceed to applications or referrals. It then details the application case lifecycle: manual or online creation, client search/match and registration, case records, dates, ownership, program timers, evidence brokering, PCR assessment, workflows, and ongoing management of clients, programs, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, notes, and timers.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section overviews Cúram Intake application case lifecycle management: adding programs and clients, search/match and prospect registration, eligibility checks, denying/withdrawing/reopening programs, timer behavior, authorization and evidence transfer to ongoing cases, PCR assessment, contact records (meetings, notes, attachments), appeals, case closure, ownership, and status transitions. It explains worker actions, configuration-dependent behavior, and how application case data flows into integrated cases/product deliveries.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-40
children.summary: Cúram Intake overview covering application and program statuses, issue handling, referrals, and worker tools. It explains Ready for Determination and Closed statuses, program states like Pending, Approved, Denied, Withdrawn, and Authorization Failed/In Progress. It describes how verification or evidence issues affect cases, issue ownership, and resubmission. It also covers creating and managing referrals to providers or agencies, plus tools such as Advisor, Notes, Application Search, My Applications, Referral Search, My Referrals, and My Programs Due.

Cúram Intake edition for versions 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2. Introduces the intake framework and No Wrong Door model across triage, screening, applications, and referrals. Covers client identification, triage and screening outcomes, application case lifecycle and statuses, eligibility/authorization, PCR assessment, evidence and issue handling, referrals to providers/agencies, and worker tools such as Advisor, Notes, Application Search, My Applications, Referral Search, My Referrals, and My Programs Due.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Copyright and legal notices for sample programs and trademarks. States that example names in the document are fictitious, sample code may be copied/modified/distributed for API-conforming development, but is provided “AS IS” without warranty or liability. Includes the Merative privacy policy link and lists trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section lists legal notices for sample data and programs, privacy policy reference, and trademark ownership. It states that example names are fictitious, sample code is provided under the Merative Client Agreement “as is” without warranty, and identifies trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, plus a copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

Merative publication notices and usage terms. Covers personal and commercial reproduction rules, preservation of proprietary notices, restrictions on derivative works and distribution outside an enterprise, and no implied rights beyond those expressly granted. Includes export-law compliance, an “AS IS” warranty disclaimer, patent notice, third-party product and website disclaimer, and copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Intake guide. Lists the main chapter on Cúram Intake overview and subsections covering identifying and triaging clients, screening for program eligibility, the application case lifecycle, managing intake issues, referrals to external agencies, and additional worker tools such as Advisor, Notes, Application Search, My Applications, Referral Search, My Referrals, My Programs Due, and Notices. Also includes front matter entries like Note, Edition, Privacy policy, and Trademarks.

children.title: 1 Cúram Intake overview
1.1 Cúram Intake Overview
What is Cúram Intake?
No Wrong Door
children.summary: Explains Cúram Intake’s “No Wrong Door” approach: a universal gateway for clients to access benefits and services without knowing the right program upfront. Workers use intake to identify needs and route clients to screening, applications, referrals, or external/community services. The section also notes intake can cover multiple programs at once, reducing duplicate contacts and repeated information capture, and introduces Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) for differential response based on client situation.

Cúram Intake is the system’s intake framework for triage, screening, applications, and referrals, including multi-program intake to reduce repeated contacts and duplicate data capture. It replaces siloed intake by giving workers a cohesive way to manage claims and service access across agencies. The section introduces the “No Wrong Door” model, where clients come with a need rather than a program name, and workers route them to eligibility screening, applications, external/community referrals, or other support, using Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) for differentiated response.

children.title: Addressing the Client's Needs
Identifying the Client
children.summary: This section explains that when a client approaches the organization, the worker first searches to see whether the person is already an existing client. If the client is new, the worker registers them and then continues with the intake process.

children.title: Determining a Client's Needs using Triage
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram Intake determines a client’s needs through triage, screening, application processing, and referrals. Triage quickly identifies immediate needs and suggests suitable services. Screening uses high-level questions and eligibility rules to assess potential program eligibility via IEG-guided questions. It also describes application processing: submitting evidence, creating an application case, matching clients, managing verifications, and authorizing programs across multiple channels. Finally, it covers referrals to external providers or agencies, including system-recommended referrals after triage.

children.title: Screening Clients to Determine Potential Eligibility for Programs
children.summary: This section explains screening in Cúram Intake: workers ask high-level questions, and eligibility rules plus IEG-driven screening determine whether a client is potentially eligible for programs. It sits alongside triage, application processing, and referrals in the intake flow. Screening results help clients decide whether to proceed with applications.

children.title: Processing Client Applications for Programs
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram Intake processes client applications for programs. It covers triage for identifying immediate needs, screening clients for potential eligibility using high-level questions and IEG-configured rules, and the application process for filing claims. Applications can be submitted in person, by phone, online, or by post. It also describes the application case used to manage evidence, verifications, client matching, and authorization through completion, plus referrals to external services or benefits during intake.

children.title: Referring a Client for Services Or Benefits
children.summary: This section explains referrals in Cúram Intake: workers can send an email referral to an external provider or agency when a client needs services or benefits. Referrals can be created at any stage of intake, including during application or screening, and the system may recommend services after triage. It highlights that referrals are for externally provided services/programs and are based on client needs identified during the intake process.

This section explains that Cúram Intake has a flexible, optional intake flow based on client needs. Workers may use triage to identify needs, then screening to assess potential eligibility for internal or external programs, or proceed directly to application if the client already knows the program. It also notes common need categories (food, shelter, safety, health, finance, education), supports referrals and recorded incoming referrals, and begins with identifying whether the client is existing or new, registering new clients as needed.

Cúram Intake is a flexible intake framework supporting triage, screening, applications, and referrals, including multi-program intake to reduce repeated client contacts and duplicate data capture. It replaces siloed processes with a “No Wrong Door” model, letting workers start from the client’s needs and route them to screening, application, or external/community referral. It also uses Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) for differentiated response. The section introduces client need categories, handling of incoming referrals, and identifying/registering new clients.

children.title: 1.2 Identifying the Client
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Intake workflow: a worker first searches for an existing client or registers a new one, possibly as a prospect person with minimal data before full registration later. Explains that person search spans persons and prospect persons, and that intake records such as triages, screenings, applications, and referrals are tied to the participant record. Also begins the triage concept, showing how core needs (food, shelter, health, education, finance, safety) are assessed to quickly identify suitable services and programs.

children.title: Searching for a Client
children.summary: This section explains the first intake step: searching for an existing client or registering a new one. Workers can search across all persons and prospect persons, review matches, and select an existing record if found. If the client is not fully known, they can be registered as a prospect with minimal data and completed later as a full person registration. It also notes that intake items like triages, screenings, application cases, forms, and referrals are linked to the participant record.

children.title: Registering a Client
children.summary: Explains the first intake step: search for an existing client or register a new one. Workers can register a client as a person or, more commonly initially, as a prospect person with minimal data, then complete full registration later when more information is available. It notes that intake records such as triages, screenings, application cases/forms, and referrals attach to the participant record. This section is useful for understanding client lookup and registration options before proceeding with intake.

Introduces the first intake step: identifying the client by searching for an existing person or registering a new one. If the client is not fully known, the worker can create a prospect person with minimal data and complete registration later. Searches span both persons and prospect persons, and intake records such as triages, screenings, applications/cases, forms, and referrals are linked to the participant record.

children.title: 1.3 Triaging a Client and Using the Results to Address Client Needs
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the intake workflow: workers first search for an existing client or register a new one, either as a full person or a prospect with minimal data for later completion. It explains person search across registered persons and prospect persons, and notes that intake artifacts like triages, screenings, applications, and referrals attach to the participant record. It also begins the triage topic, describing how workers assess basic needs and receive suitable services, providers, and programs based on the client’s situation.

children.title: Running a Triage
children.summary: Explains how workers run a triage in Cúram Intake to quickly assess a client’s needs using a short multiple-choice question set for prospect or registered people. Triage cannot be saved midstream and must be completed or discarded. Results are generated by a Cúram Eligibility Rule set and may recommend community services, providers, programs, and urgent alerts. The section also notes triage history retention, alert text behavior, and mapping recommended services to providers.

children.title: Triage Results
Alert Text
children.summary: Explains how triage results can display alert text based on client answers. Alerts are configured in the triage rule set and shown on the triage results tab to warn workers about important issues, such as emergency needs requiring immediate action. Multiple alerts may appear for a single triage depending on responses.

children.title: Mapping Services to Providers
Viewing Provider Details
children.summary: This section explains that Cúram Intake shows provider details by letting workers select a provider on the map and view more information. The map displays providers relevant to the agency and client location, and workers can search by area (for example, Springfield) to display matching providers for selected services. It also notes that provider mapping and recommended services require Provider Management to be installed and configured.

This section explains how Cúram Intake maps recommended community services to service providers during triage results. It highlights that a limited set of preselected services is shown initially to avoid overcrowding the map, helping workers quickly choose an appropriate provider for the client’s basic needs.

children.title: Referring Clients to Community Services
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Intake helps workers refer clients to community service providers when agency programs are unavailable or the client is ineligible. Referrals are created from triage results, pre-populated with provider/client details, and can be sent, edited, or deleted before sending by email. Also introduces nearby provider mapping and viewing provider details, plus related capability to recommend government programs, including internal screening/applications and external agency referrals. Ends by noting the advisor provides context-sensitive help during intake.

children.title: Recommending Government Programs to Clients
children.summary: This section explains how Intake recommends government programs after triage. It describes how CER rules identify eligible internal programs and external agency programs based on client answers. Workers can screen or apply for internal programs, or screen and create external referrals using a 2-step wizard prefilled with program and client contact details. It also notes that unsent referrals can be sent, edited, or deleted, and points to related screening, application, and referral process sections.

children.title: Providing Context Sensitive Help To Case Workers
children.summary: This section explains the advisor feature for intake workers: a context-sensitive help tool that shows tips and reminders during the intake process. When opened from triage, it uses entered triage data to present advice about areas needing attention, helping guide next steps. It is useful for understanding how Cúram supports workers with decision guidance during triage-driven intake.

children.title: Triage Notes
children.summary: Explains triage notes in Cúram Intake: workers can add and maintain notes tied to a specific triage record from the triage results tab. Also briefly relates triage to screening for eligibility, where workers assess potential program eligibility using configured questions, see recommended programs and alerts, and may then apply, refer, or handle emergencies. Screening is optional and can be accessed from triage recommendations or skipped entirely.

Explains triage results in Cúram Intake: after a short question set, the system uses CER rules to show recommended community services, providers, government programs, and urgent alerts. Workers can create, edit, send, or delete unsent referrals to community service providers or external agencies, and screen/apply for internal programs. It also covers provider mapping and details, preselected service display, a triage history record, and context-sensitive advisor help based on triage answers.

Explains how Cúram Intake handles triaging a client after initial search or registration. Workers can triage prospect or registered persons using a short multiple-choice question set that cannot be saved midstream. CER rules generate results showing recommended community services, providers, government programs, and urgent alerts, plus a triage history record. The section also covers mapping services to providers, viewing provider details, creating/editing/sending referrals to community or external agencies, screening/applying for programs, and advisor help based on triage answers.

children.title: 1.4 Screening Clients to Determine Potential Eligibility for Programs
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces screening clients for potential program eligibility in Cúram Intake. Workers can screen prospect or registered persons using organization-configured high-level questions, and the system runs eligibility rules to show recommended government programs and possible urgent alerts. After screening, workers can apply for programs, create external referrals, or respond to emergencies. The section also notes that screening history is saved and accessible later, and that Cúram provides the infrastructure but no preconfigured screening scripts or rules.

children.title: Screening a Client for Potential Eligibility
Screening for a Specific Program or a Range of Programs
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Intake handles screening for one or multiple programs. Workers may choose a screening category when multiple screenings are configured, or go directly to a new screening for a single option. The section covers selecting programs, using IEG question scripts, saving or discarding in-progress screenings, and preventing duplicate in-progress records. It also describes configurable program-specific business rules and eligibility rules, and how screening results show potential eligibility, short explanations, and urgent alerts to help decide whether to proceed with intake or application.

children.title: Programs that Clients can be Screened For
children.summary: This section explains that organizations can configure which programs clients may be screened for, using one or multiple screening categories. Workers select programs, answer guided IEG questions, and configured business rules/CERs determine potential eligibility. It notes that screening results are only indicators, may include urgent alerts, and can lead to proceeding with an application or being referred elsewhere. It also mentions saving, resuming, or discarding in-progress screenings, and preventing duplicate in-progress records for the same program.

children.title: Capturing Client Information for a Screening
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Intake captures client information during screening using IEG scripts. Workers answer sequential, categorized question groups after selecting one or more programs, with screening able to be submitted once minimum required data is entered. It also covers how configured business rules and CERs use the collected answers to determine potential program eligibility, display results and urgent alerts, and support single vs. multiple screening setups.

Explains how workers screen prospect or registered clients for potential eligibility using configurable high-level questions and eligibility rules. Screening can be started from triage recommendations or directly, is optional, and may be skipped or resumed later. Results show recommended programs and possible urgent alerts, then let workers apply for programs, create external referrals, or respond to emergencies. Cúram provides the infrastructure but no preconfigured screening scripts or rules. Screening history stores date, alerts, and recommendations for completed screenings.

children.title: Recommending Programs for Clients based on Screening Results
Applying for Programs based on Screening Results
children.summary: Explains how, after screening, a worker can use results to apply for in-agency programs, create referrals to external agencies, or handle emergencies. It covers the screening results list, which shows available programs and potential eligibility, plus the option to take an application or create a 2-step referral wizard. Also notes alert text for urgent needs and screening-specific notes. Introduces the lifecycle of a client application case, including required application information, configurable timers/eligibility/PCR assessments, appeals, and common ways cases are created.

children.title: Alert Text
children.summary: This section explains that screening results may trigger alerts for workers, including urgent emergency warnings such as advising immediate emergency services. It notes that multiple alerts can appear based on the client’s responses.

children.title: Screening Notes
children.summary: This section notes that after screening, workers can act on results by applying for in-agency programs, creating referrals to external agencies, or handling emergencies. It explains that screening results may show potential eligibility or undetermined eligibility, and that external referrals use a 2-step wizard. It also highlights alert text for urgent client needs and the ability to capture and maintain notes tied specifically to a screening record.

Explains how screening results are shown after a worker completes a program screening. Results indicate potential eligibility, include a short eligibility explanation, and are generated by CER rules after submission. The section emphasizes that results are not a guarantee of eligibility and may include urgent alerts. It also notes that based on results, a worker may proceed with an application for configured programs or refer the client elsewhere if they do not continue.

Explains how workers screen prospect or registered clients in Cúram Intake using organization-configured high-level questions and eligibility rules to identify potential program eligibility. Screening can start from triage or directly, may be skipped, resumed, or discarded, and supports single or multiple screening categories. Results show potential eligibility, brief explanations, and possible urgent alerts, then allow applications, referrals, or emergency response. Screening history is saved. Cúram provides the screening infrastructure, but agencies must configure their own scripts and rules.

children.title: 1.5 Lifecycle of a Client's Application Case: From Creation to Closure
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces post-screening actions in Cúram Intake: workers can apply for agency programs, create referrals to external agencies, or respond to emergencies. It explains that screening results show potentially eligible programs and whether eligibility could be determined, and that applications or referrals can be initiated from those results. It also notes alert text for urgent client issues and the ability to record screening-specific notes. The excerpt then begins the application case lifecycle, describing application cases as the central record for applications and referrals, with configurable timers, eligibility checks, PCR assessments, and appeals.

children.title: Creating an Application Case
Creating an Application Case Using an Application Form
children.summary: Explains how workers create application cases using an application form (IEG script) in Cúram. Covers where the form can be launched, selecting forms/programs, validation to prevent duplicate pending applications, capturing required information, saving/resuming/deleting in-progress forms, reference number generation, submission rules and optional submission scripts, viewing in-progress/completed forms, and creation of a PDF attachment on the resulting application case.

children.title: Creating an Application Case Directly Without Using an Application Form
children.summary: Explains how workers can create an application case directly without an application form, based on configuration. The worker selects the case type, can adjust the default application date, and may record special assistance needs, how the application was received, and preferred contact method. Saving triggers case creation, after which the worker adds programs, additional clients, and evidence. Also notes that online applications submitted via Universal Access can invoke the same intake process, with configuration determining whether Cúram or another system handles them, and whether processing is manual or automatic.

children.title: Receiving an Online Application
children.summary: This section explains how online applications submitted through Universal Access are received and processed in Cúram Intake. It notes that organizations can configure whether online submissions are handled in Cúram or an external system. If Cúram is the intake system, submission triggers application case creation, after which the case can be processed either manually by a worker or automatically by the system, depending on configuration.

Explains how application cases are created in Cúram: manually by workers, via an application form (IEG script), or automatically from Universal Access online submissions when Cúram is the intake system. Covers form launch points, program selection, duplicate-pending validation, saving/resuming/deleting, submission rules, reference numbers, and generated PDF attachments. Also describes direct case creation without a form, including setting application date, special assistance, received method, and contact preference, then adding programs, clients, and evidence.

children.title: Application Case Creation System Processing
Searching, Matching and Registering Clients
children.summary: Describes how Cúram Intake searches, matches, and registers clients during application case creation. It explains when client matching is triggered (online citizen applications, prospect persons, registered persons with additional clients, and direct prospect case creation), how configurable search criteria produce match scores and classify results as conclusive, inconclusive, or no match, and that a conclusive match adds the person as a case participant. If no match or matching is disabled, the system determines whether to register the client as a prospect or person based on the application’s Client Registration setting.

children.title: Creating the Application Case Records
children.summary: This section explains how application case records are created after clients are matched or registered. It covers how the system determines the application case type from program configuration, including when multiple cases or a single case are created, and notes that directly created cases require worker selection. It also describes setting the case to Open, how application dates are assigned for online, internal, or direct applications, how case and application references are generated, and that all clients are recorded as case participants.

children.title: Adding Programs and Setting Program Timers
children.summary: Explains how requested programs are added to an application case when it is created from an online or internal application: each program starts as Pending with the application date recorded, while worker-created cases require manual program addition. Describes program timer configuration, including start dates based on either the application date or the program-added date. Also notes automatic evidence addition via the Data Mapping Engine and Evidence Broker, including configurable handling of brokered evidence.

children.title: Adding Evidence Automatically to the Application Case
children.summary: Explains how evidence is automatically added to an application case. Evidence can come from the Cúram Data Mapping Engine when an IEG script captures data, or from Cúram Evidence Broker via configured brokering from other case types, including Person/Prospect Person evidence. When the application case is created and participants are added, relevant active evidence may be brokered to it. Configurations control whether brokered evidence is automatically accepted and activated, added as in-edit evidence, or added as incoming evidence for worker acceptance.

children.title: Assessing the Priority, Complexity and Risk of the Application Case
children.summary: This section explains how application cases are assessed and processed. It covers Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR), where a configured ruleset runs when the case is created to rate the case and may trigger a PCR workflow. It also describes two creation-time workflows: Application Case Created (for tasks/notifications) and Straight Through (auto-authorizes programs and creates the ongoing case, only when all clients are fully registered and for online/internal submissions). It notes workers can manage clients, programs, timers, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, PCR, and notes.

children.title: Invoking Workflows
children.summary: This section explains workflow invocation options when an application case is created. It covers the Application Case Created workflow, which can trigger tasks or notifications on case creation, and the Straight Through workflow, which can automatically authorize programs and create the ongoing case only when all clients are fully registered. It also notes that straight-through processing applies only to online submissions or forms entered by an internal worker, not directly created application cases. The section briefly introduces worker management of case information after creation.

Describes application case creation processing in Cúram Intake. Covers client search/matching and registration rules, including conclusive/inconclusive/no match outcomes and when prospects or persons are added as participants. Explains how case type, status, application date, references, owner, and participants are set. Also details automatic program association and timers for online/internal submissions, manual program addition for direct cases, and automatic evidence addition via Data Mapping Engine or Evidence Broker, including configurable acceptance/activation handling.

children.title: Managing Application Case Information
children.summary: Explains how workers manage application case information after creation, including viewing, adding, removing, and updating clients, programs, timers, evidence, eligibility checks, related cases, PCR, and notes. Notes that Person/Prospect Person evidence should be brokered as incoming evidence for worker review rather than automatically accepted. Also covers PCR assessment via configured rulesets at creation, and two optional workflows: Application Case Created and Straight Through, with Straight Through only for fully registered online or worker-submitted applications.

children.title: Adding a Program to an Application Case
children.summary: Explains how workers add programs to an application case after creation. At least one program is required; if only one program exists, it is added automatically. Workers can manually add only programs not already associated or withdrawn, choosing the program type and add date. Program timers start automatically when added. Also introduces related client management on application cases, including registering clients as persons or prospects and resolving potential matches from search and match.

children.title: Managing the Clients on an Application Case
Selecting a Potential Match for a Client
children.summary: Explains how application case clients are handled when search and match returns multiple conclusive or inconclusive matches. The client is registered as a prospect, and the worker must review potential matches to decide whether one is a true match. Selecting a potential match replaces the prospect with the matched person as the case participant on the application case. Useful for understanding prospect resolution and match selection during application case creation.

children.title: Finding a Match for a Client
children.summary: Explains how a worker can manually find and match a client to an existing person when automatic matching fails or is unavailable, and what happens when a match is confirmed. Also covers registering a client as a new person with minimum required data, adding additional clients to an application case either by selecting an existing person or creating one, and removing clients before authorization. Removed clients become previous clients; matching or registering transfers prospect data and evidence to the person record.

children.title: Registering a Client as a Person
children.summary: Explains how a prospect client is converted into a registered person when no match is found, using minimum required details (first name, last name, date of birth, gender), and how the new person inherits the prospect’s case evidence and data. Also covers adding additional clients to an application case by selecting an existing person or registering a new one, and removing clients before authorization, which ends their case participant record and moves them to the Previous Clients list.

children.title: Adding a Client to an Application Case
children.summary: Explains how to add additional clients to an application case before authorization. A worker can either search for and select an existing registered person, or create a new person using minimum required details (first name, last name, date of birth, gender), which then adds them as a case participant. Also notes clients may be removed before authorization, ending their case participant record and moving them to Previous Clients.

children.title: Removing a Client from an Application Case
children.summary: This section explains that clients can be removed from an application case only before any programs are authorized. It gives the use case of incorrect or extra household members added to an online application. Removing a client ends the associated case participant record, and the removed client is moved to the Previous Clients list on the application case.

Explains how application case clients must be registered as persons before processing. Covers automatic search and match, handling prospects when matches are inconclusive, selecting a potential match, manually finding a match, or registering a new person with minimum data (first name, last name, date of birth, gender). Also describes adding additional clients before authorization, either by selecting an existing person or creating one, and removing clients before authorization, which moves them to Previous Clients.

children.title: Checking Eligibility on an Application Case
children.summary: Describes eligibility checking and program management on an application case in Cúram Intake. Workers can run eligibility checks after prospects are registered and at least one program exists, view rule-set results based on current evidence, repeat checks, and review all prior checks. Organizations may configure checks across all programs on the case type. The section also covers managing programs: denying, withdrawing, reopening, and editing added dates. Withdrawal can be manual or online, with online requests automatically updating status to Withdrawn and notifying the case owner.

children.title: Managing Programs on an Application Case
Denying a Program
children.summary: This section explains program management on an application case, focusing on denying a program. A worker may deny a program if authorization failed, the client is ineligible, or the client already receives the benefit. The worker must enter a denial reason and optional comments, and can do this at any time before case closure. It also briefly covers related actions: checking eligibility, withdrawing programs, and withdrawing programs online via Universal Access.

children.title: Withdrawing a Program
Withdrawing a Program Online
children.summary: Explains online program withdrawal in Cúram Intake. When a client submits a withdrawal request through Universal Access, the system automatically changes the program status on the application case to Withdrawn and notifies the application case owner. This section sits within broader program management on an application case, alongside eligibility checks, denial, withdrawal, and reopening actions.

Describes how workers can withdraw a program from an application case at a client’s request, requiring the withdrawal date, requester, reason, and method (phone, in person, or paper). Also covers online withdrawal: when a client submits a withdrawal request through Universal Access, Cúram Intake automatically changes the program status to Withdrawn and notifies the application case owner. Useful for understanding program management actions on an application case.

children.title: Reopening a Program
children.summary: Explains how a denied or withdrawn program on an application case can be reopened when configured, including required reopen date, reason, and comments. Reopening returns the program to Pending, reopens a Closed case to Open, resumes any timer based on configuration, and can trigger a workflow. Also covers viewing history of reopen/denial/withdrawal events and managing application timers: automatic creation, expiry calculation, warning periods, overdue status, timer extension, and approval workflow.

children.title: Viewing Reopen, Denial and Withdrawal Details
children.summary: This section explains how workers view reopen, denial, and withdrawal details for a program on an application case, including the maintained history of multiple events. It also describes reopening a denied or withdrawn program: configurable permission, required reopen date/reason/comments, status changes back to Pending (and case Open if needed), timer resumption behavior, and optional reopen workflow. It additionally introduces application timers: configuration options, automatic creation, expiry/warning/overdue behavior, timer lists, and extension with possible supervisor approval.

This section covers program management on an application case: checking client eligibility for associated programs, denying a program before case closure when a client is ineligible or already receiving the benefit, and withdrawing a program at the client’s request with required date/reason/requester/method details. It also describes online withdrawal via Universal Access, where the program status is automatically set to Withdrawn and the case owner is notified. It notes that removing a client does not automatically remove related evidence.

children.title: Managing Timers
children.summary: This section explains application timers for programs on an application case. It covers configurable timer settings such as duration, business vs. calendar days, warning periods, extension approval, start date, and related workflows. Timers are created automatically when a program is added, calculate an expiry date, can enter warning or overdue status, and are shown in a list with status icons. It also notes that timers can be extended, with optional supervisor approval.

children.title: Authorizing Programs on an Application Case
Authorizing at the Application Level
children.summary: Describes application-level authorization for intake cases, used when multiple programs on an application are authorized together. Explains that the organization can configure whether authorization creates or reuses an integrated case and/or outcome plan, or lets the worker choose, and can specify the case/plan types. On “Authorize,” the system checks configuration, prompts for confirmation and possible selection/creation, then validates evidence; if validation fails, authorization stops and eligibility is determined by the authorization rule set.

children.title: Authorizing at the Program Level
children.summary: Describes program-level authorization when an application case has one or more program types and separate integrated cases or outcome plans manage ongoing work. Authorization can be manual and does not require a rule set. Configuration determines whether a new or existing integrated case/outcome plan is used and what type is created. The process identifies required evidence, validates it, and may create/select the ongoing case or plan, copy evidence, activate it, mark approved programs as Approved, and save authorization details.

children.title: Invoking Authorization as part of a Straight Through Process
children.summary: Describes how authorization proceeds in a straight-through workflow: steps execute automatically until worker input is needed, then pause and resume when input is provided. Covers creating or reusing an integrated case/outcome plan, copying and validating required evidence, and updating program status to Approved. Notes that evidence may be copied only if new or changed, and that notifications are sent if validation fails. Also summarizes result handling, including retry/deny options and configurable notifications.

children.title: Copying Evidence to an Ongoing Case and Outcome Plan
children.summary: Explains how, during authorization, evidence required by an ongoing integrated case or outcome plan is copied from the application case, then automatically validated and activated. If a new ongoing case/plan is created, all application clients are added and the primary client is fixed; if an existing case is used, only missing clients are added. Notes that copied evidence may be skipped if already copied and unchanged, and that authorization continues even if evidence copy/validation fails, with notifications sent. Also covers approval status and result notifications.

children.title: Reviewing the Authorization Results
children.summary: This section explains how workers review program authorization outcomes in Cúram Intake. It describes notifications for successful and failed authorizations, including that approved programs are marked Approved while failed ones remain Pending. It notes workers can retry authorization or deny a program after failure, and that organizations can configure these notifications on or off.

Explains how intake authorizes programs on an application case, either at the application level or per program, with configurable creation/reuse of integrated cases and outcome plans. Covers the authorization steps: confirm action, validate application evidence, determine required evidence, create/select the ongoing case or plan, copy and activate evidence, update status to Approved, and save event details. Also notes straight-through authorization pauses for worker input, copied evidence is only re-copied when changed, and failure leaves programs Pending with configurable notifications and retry/deny options.

children.title: Assessing Priority, Complexity, and Risk for an Application Case
children.summary: Explains application-case Priority, Complexity, Risk (PCR) assessment and contact management. PCR is defined as priority (time to deal with the case), complexity (how involved the client’s situation is), and risk (potential organizational impact). If configured, PCR is calculated throughout the application lifecycle by a ruleset triggered at creation and on events such as evidence activation, client or program changes, eligibility checks, or prospect matching, with optional manual invocation. PCR changes can trigger workflow actions. Also covers managing client contact: meetings, minutes, notes, attachments, and communications.

children.title: Managing Contact with the Client
Meetings
children.summary: This section explains that workers can schedule meetings with clients to complete application case information. Meetings may be mandatory before program authorization for some programs, or only needed when there is missing or conflicting information. It sits within managing client contact, alongside meeting minutes, notes, attachments, and communications. For scheduling details, it refers readers to the Cúram Calendaring Guide.

children.title: Meeting Minutes
children.summary: This section covers meeting minutes functionality for application cases: recording meeting details, notes, decisions, attendees, actions, attachments, and related cases, with email distribution to attendees and others. It also describes case notes history and attaching supporting documents like Word, Excel, PDF, and scanned evidence with security-controlled access. Additional topics include managing appeals for denied, pending, or approved programs, appeal case types and workflow, and how application cases close when all programs are completed, with possible reopening after denial or withdrawal is reversed.

children.title: Notes
children.summary: This section explains application-case notes. Workers can capture and maintain notes specific to an application case, with a history of notes retained. It also briefly references related case functions in the surrounding page, including meeting minutes, attachments, appeals, and case closure, but the focus here is on notes tied to an application case.

children.title: Attachments
children.summary: This section explains attachments on an application case: workers can add supplemental files such as scanned birth certificates or bank statements, with support for Word, Excel, and PDF. File size is not restricted by the system, though agencies may set a limit, and attached files can be accessed by authorized users. The page also briefly introduces adjacent topics in the chapter, including meeting minutes, notes, appealing a program, and closing an application case.

This section describes how workers manage client contact on an application case, including meetings, meeting minutes, notes, attachments, and communications. It explains that meetings can be used to complete case information and may be mandatory before authorization for some programs or needed only when information is missing or conflicting. It also notes that meeting scheduling details are covered in the Cúram Calendaring Guide.

children.title: Appealing a Program
children.summary: Describes how workers manage program appeals on an application case. A program that is denied, pending, or approved may be appealed depending on configuration, and one appeal can cover one or more programs or be added to an existing appeal case. An appeal creates an appeal case to manage statements, hearings, decision review, and implementation. The section also notes appeal types: hearing case, hearing review, and judicial review, with processes configured in Administration.

children.title: Closing an Application Case
children.summary: This section explains when an application case is automatically closed and when it can reopen. A case is set to Closed once all programs are completed as Approved, Denied, or Withdrawn. If programs are approved, integrated cases and product deliveries handle ongoing eligibility and benefit delivery. The case can return to Open if a denied or withdrawn program is reopened by a worker, such as after an error or a successful appeal.

children.title: Application Case Ownership
children.summary: This section explains application case ownership configuration in Cúram Intake. It describes assigning cases to organization objects such as a user, organization unit, position, or work queue, enabling shared visibility and task access, and notes that ownership can also be manually set to individual users or groups. It also outlines application case lifecycle statuses and transitions, including Created, Awaiting Resolution, Open, Authorization In Progress, and Authorization Failed, with the conditions that trigger each status.

children.title: Application Case and Program Statuses
Setting the Status to Ready For Determination
children.summary: Explains the optional, configurable “Ready for Determination” application case status. Workers can manually set an Open application case to this status once information gathering is complete, indicating the case is ready for authorization or denial decisions. The status is useful when one group collects application information and another makes determination decisions. Notes that if this status is enabled, an application case must be Ready for Determination before it can be closed; otherwise only Open cases can close.

Describes application case ownership and lifecycle statuses, including how cases can be assigned to users or organizational units for shared visibility. Summarizes application case statuses such as Created, Awaiting Resolution, Open, Authorization In Progress, Authorization Failed, Ready for Determination, and Closed, plus program statuses like Pending, Approved, Denied, Withdrawn, and related authorization states. Explains the optional Ready for Determination status, used when information gathering is complete and a separate worker group makes authorization/denial decisions; if enabled, cases must reach this status before closing.

Describes the full lifecycle of a Cúram application case: creation from screening results, direct entry, forms, or online Universal Access submissions; client registration and matching; adding programs and evidence; eligibility checks, timers, PCR assessment, contact/notes, and appeals; authorization and status handling; and closure/reopening rules. Also covers ownership configuration and key application case/program statuses, including optional Ready for Determination.

children.title: 1.6 Managing Intake Issues
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces intake issue handling for application cases. It explains that verification rules processing errors are logged to the PIE queue, leave the case in Created status, and require an agency worker to fix and retry before submission can continue. It also covers evidence validation issues, which can place a case in Awaiting Resolution until corrected and resubmitted. Finally, it describes how application cases are assigned to an issues owner or default owner, depending on whether the application is online or internal and on system configuration.

children.title: Verification Rules Processing
children.summary: This section explains how application submission handles verification and validation issues. Verification ruleset failures during evidence processing are logged in the Process Instance Errors queue, leave the application case in Created status, and require an agency worker to fix and retry. Evidence validation issues can place the case in Awaiting Resolution until corrected and resubmitted. It also describes how an application case is assigned to an issues owner or default owner, depending on whether the application is online or internal and on system configuration.

children.title: Evidence Validation Issues
children.summary: This section explains evidence validation issues during application case submission. If validation fails when adding evidence, the case moves to Awaiting Resolution, and the case worker must manually fix the evidence and resubmit before processing can continue. It also describes how an issue owner may be assigned: online applications route to a configured issues owner or default owner, while internal applications may be assigned to the creator, issues owner, or default owner depending on configuration.

children.title: Application Case Authorization Failures
children.summary: If application case authorization fails (for example, evidence brokering cannot broker evidence to the integrated case), the case status becomes “Authorization Failed.” The worker can resubmit after fixing the underlying issues. The section also begins the topic of referrals: what a referral is, when workers create them during intake, and how to create referrals to community service providers or external agencies from screening, triage, the referrals list, or client home page.

Covers how intake issues are handled during application submission and authorization. Verification rules processing errors are logged in the PIE queue, leave the application case in Created status, and must be fixed and retried by an agency worker. Evidence validation failures move the case to Awaiting Resolution until corrected and resubmitted. It also explains how an application case is assigned an issues owner or default owner, with different routing for online vs. internal applications based on configuration.

children.title: 1.7 Referring a Client to External Agencies and Service Providers
What is a Referral?
children.summary: Defines a referral as a document sent to an external provider or agency to inform them a client needs their services or benefits. Explains that workers can create referrals at any point in intake, commonly after triage, screening, or application. Covers referral creation from the referrals list/client home page, screening results, or triage results, and describes selecting community service providers or external agencies, entering client details, and storing the referral creation date.

children.title: Creating a Referral
Selecting a Service Provider
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Intake supports creating referrals to service providers and external agencies. Community providers can be selected from a triage map, which pre-populates the referral form; the client’s first and last name must be entered before saving. Referrals can also be created from the referrals list by searching for a provider, entering client contact details, and saving. For external agencies, the worker searches and selects an agency, enters client details, and creates the referral. Referral creation date is stored.

children.title: Selecting an External Agency
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Intake supports referrals to external agencies and service providers. Defines a referral as a document sent to an outside provider/agency when a client needs services or benefits. Describes when referrals are commonly created (after triage, screening, or application) and where they can be initiated (referrals list/client home page, screening results, triage results). For external agencies, workers search for and select an agency, then enter the client’s contact details; the referral creation date is stored.

children.title: Viewing a List of Open Referrals
children.summary: Shows how workers view a list of a client’s open community service and external agency referrals. The list displays referral details and allows the worker to edit, delete, send, or create a new referral. It also explains what happens after creation: if the provider/agency has an email address, the referral is sent automatically; otherwise it remains unsent and can be printed for delivery or mailed.

Explains how Cúram Intake creates referrals to community service providers and external agencies during intake, usually after triage, screening, or application. A referral is a document sent to an outside provider/agency for needed services or benefits. Referrals can be initiated from the referrals list/client home page, screening results, or triage results. Community providers may be selected from a triage map, which pre-populates the form; the client’s first and last name must be entered before saving. External agencies are searched and selected, then client contact details are entered. Referral creation date is stored.

children.title: What Happens after a Referral is Created?
children.summary: Explains what happens after a referral is created: once a community service or external agency is recorded, the system sends the referral by email if an address exists. If no email is on file, the referral remains unsent and the worker must print it for the client to deliver or send it by post. Also notes the worker can view, edit, delete, send, or create referrals from the open referrals list.

Explains what a referral is in Cúram Intake and when workers create one during intake, typically after triage, screening, or application. Describes referral creation from the referrals list/client home page, screening results, or triage results. Covers two paths: selecting a community service provider (often from a triage map, which pre-populates the form) or searching for an external agency. Notes the worker must enter the client’s name/contact details and that the referral creation date is stored.

children.title: 1.8 Additional Tools for Assisting Workers
Introduction
children.summary: This section introduces additional tools that support workers during intake. It briefly covers viewing open referrals and what happens after a referral is created, including sending by email, printing, or posting if no email exists. It then introduces available tools: Advisor, Notes, Application search, and My Applications view. The main focus is the Advisor, a CER-based, context-sensitive tool that gives tips, reminders, and links to relevant tasks during intake and evidence capture, with configurable rules and text.

children.title: Advisor
children.summary: Advisor is a dynamic, context-sensitive tool in Cúram Intake that gives workers tips and reminders during the intake process. Built on the Cúram Express Rules (CER) engine, it analyzes entered or existing client data and can link workers directly to areas needing attention. It supports intake step guidance and evidence capture, including highlighting issues such as missing mandatory data. Advisor rules and displayed text are organization-configurable.

children.title: Notes
children.summary: This section describes Cúram Intake utilities for worker case management: capturing notes during intake, application search and “My Applications,” referral search and “My Referrals,” and “My Programs Due.” It explains where notes can be entered, that note history is retained, what search criteria are available for applications and referrals, which case statuses appear in the “My” views, and how workers can filter due/overdue pending programs by days remaining.

children.title: Application Search
children.summary: Cúram Intake’s Application Search section explains how workers find and manage workload items. It covers searching application cases by reference, type, status, received date, and client; the My Applications view for the current worker’s owned cases in key statuses; referral search and My Referrals with similar criteria and statuses; and My Programs Due for locating pending programs due soon or overdue, filtered by days remaining.

children.title: My Applications
children.summary: This section describes Cúram Intake worker views and search tools for managing cases and workload. It covers notes capture, application search, My Applications, referral search, My Referrals, and My Programs Due. My Applications lists application cases owned by the worker with statuses such as Created, Awaiting Resolution, Open, Ready for Determination, Authorization In Progress, or Authorization Failed. It helps workers find, track, and prioritize application and referral cases, plus overdue or due programs.

children.title: Referral Search
children.summary: Cúram Intake’s referral search lets workers find referral cases by application case reference, referral reference, type, status, referral date, client, source, and referral reason. It complements related workload views like My Referrals, which lists referral application cases owned by the worker in key statuses. This section also sits alongside nearby features for notes, application search, my applications, and my programs due, all aimed at helping workers manage intake workload and track case information.

children.title: My Referrals
children.summary: Describes Cúram Intake’s My Referrals view for workers managing referral application cases. It lists referrals owned by the current worker with statuses such as Created, Awaiting Resolution, Open, Ready for Determination, Authorization In Progress, and Authorization Failed. This section sits alongside related intake features like referral search, my applications, notes, and my programs due, and helps users quickly access and track their referral workload.

children.title: My Programs Due
children.summary: Describes the “My Programs Due” page in Cúram Intake. It helps workers manage workload by listing programs owned by the current worker that are due or overdue on application cases. Search is based on days left to process; e.g., 1 shows programs due today, 5 shows programs due in 5 or fewer days. An additional filter can show overdue programs. Only programs with Pending status are returned, and search criteria are saved for the next visit.

This section covers worker-assist tools in Cúram Intake: Advisor, Notes, Application Search, My Applications, Referral Search, My Referrals, and My Programs Due. Advisor is a CER-based, context-sensitive helper that gives intake and evidence-capture tips/reminders and can link to relevant tasks; its rules and text are configurable. It also explains capturing notes, searching and tracking application/referral cases by key criteria, viewing owned cases in “My” lists by status, and finding due/overdue pending programs by days remaining.

Overview of Cúram Intake, a flexible “No Wrong Door” intake framework for triage, screening, applications, and referrals across multiple programs. It explains how workers identify/register clients (including prospect persons), assess needs with triage, determine potential eligibility with screening, manage application cases through submission and authorization, handle intake issues, and create referrals to external agencies or community providers. It also covers worker-assist tools such as Advisor, Notes, Application Search, My Applications, Referral Search, My Referrals, and My Programs Due.
